2026-04-16 22:50 · 9,141 stocks · 7196 API calls
Lynch + Buffett
🤖 AI Top Picks — Claude Sonnet
1. DOCS Doximity, Inc. · Healthcare Technology HIGH ACT NOW Quality Compounder

"The LinkedIn for doctors — 80% of US physicians are already members, and pharma can't reach them any other way"

The market is pricing DOCS at 32% of its 52-week high and PE 18.7x as if it's a generic SaaS company in secular decline. What it actually is: a near-monopoly professional network with 80%+ US physician penetration where pharma companies have no substitute for reaching verified prescribers. Revenue just re-accelerated from +13% to +20%, FCF margins are 49%, ROIC is 20%, zero debt, and 20% net cash sits idle on the balance sheet. The market is anchored on a 2023-2024 pharma ad spend freeze and has not updated for the structural recovery — this is not a cyclical recovery, it's a moat that deepened while the stock got cut in half.

Price $24.22 PEG 1.6 P/E 18.7 ROIC 20.5% Catalyst Q1 FY2027 earnings (May 2026) — the Street is modeling modest growth continuation; if management confirms pharma partner expansion and raises guidance, the PE multiple re-rates from 18.7x toward fair value of 28-32x on a 49% FCF margin business, implying 50-70% upside from current levels. Watch A structural shift in pharma marketing budgets away from digital HCP platforms toward AI-driven direct-to-consumer would break the thesis — watch for any top-10 pharma partner non-renewal or public commentary about reducing Doximity spend.
2. QLYS Qualys, Inc. · Technology — Cybersecurity HIGH ACT NOW IV Discount

"A cybersecurity cash machine trading at 15x earnings with 46% FCF margins and 28% ROIC — priced like a dying business, built like a fortress"

Qualys is the only name in this entire candidate set where four independent specialists converged with HIGH conviction, and for good reason: 83% gross margins, 46% FCF margins, 28% ROIC, 12% net cash, no debt, and a 74% DCF margin of safety — yet it sits at 54% of its 52-week high at PE 15.4x. The market has misclassified QLYS as a slow-growth legacy vendor being disrupted by CrowdStrike, when in reality its agent-free cloud architecture is technically superior for mid-market compliance scanning and its CNAPP pivot opens a $10B adjacent TAM that is not in any sell-side model. Cybersecurity spending accelerates after breach cycles, not before them, and the 2025 enterprise breach cycle is now fully matured.

Price $83.56 PEG 1.6 P/E 15.4 ROIC 28.1% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings (likely July 2026) where CNAPP/cloud security ARR growth is disclosed — if the CSPM and patch management products show accelerating attach rates, analyst upgrades follow and the multiple re-rates from 15x toward 22-25x, consistent with a 28% ROIC business with 46% FCF margins. Watch If revenue growth decelerates below 8% for two consecutive quarters, the CNAPP pivot thesis fails and this becomes a genuine slow-growth value trap — watch Q2 ARR disclosure closely for cloud security bookings.
3. HALO Halozyme Therapeutics, Inc. · Healthcare — Royalty/Licensing HIGH ACT NOW Fast Grower

"The market thinks HALO is a biotech — it's actually a royalty machine with 58% operating margins and forward PE of 7.5x"

HALO is the single most egregious misclassification in this entire candidate set. The market prices it at biotech volatility multiples (trailing PE 25.3x) while the actual business is a pure royalty and licensing platform — ENHANZE technology is licensed to 13+ Big Pharma partners including Roche, J&J, and AbbVie who bear all drug development risk. Revenue is accelerating from +22% to +38%, operating margins are 58%, FCF margins are 46%, ROIC is 24%, and the forward PE collapses to 7.5x as Janssen's subcutaneous Darzalex volume ramps with contractual royalty step-ups. At 7.5x forward earnings with 58% operating margins, this is simply mispriced.

Price $66.76 PEG 0.7 P/E 25.3 ROIC 23.6% Catalyst Darzalex SubQ volume step-up royalties in Q2 2026 report — Janssen has guided to accelerating SubQ conversion of its patient base, and each percentage point of conversion drives contracted royalty rate increases that flow directly to HALO's bottom line; one strong quarterly royalty beat should force healthcare analysts to re-model the forward PE. Watch Unexpected loss of a major partner (J&J de-prioritizing Darzalex in favor of next-gen asset) or a generic ENHANZE-equivalent approval by FDA would structurally impair the royalty stream — neither is imminent but both must be monitored.
4. PEGA Pegasystems Inc. · Technology — Enterprise Software HIGH WITHIN WEEKS Quality Compounder

"Enterprise AI workflow platform growing 17% with PEG 0.8 and 29% ROIC — priced as a stagnant legacy vendor mid-transformation"

Pega's revenue just re-accelerated from +5% to +17% and the market has not updated its mental model — it still prices PEGA as a slow legacy BPM vendor (PE 18.5x) when the business is completing a multi-year perpetual-to-ARR license conversion that mechanically depressed reported revenue for three years and is now releasing that backlog as recurring subscription growth. With PEG 0.8, ROIC of 29%, and 5-year EG estimate of 24%, this is a textbook quality compounder priced as a value stock at the exact moment the ARR flywheel becomes visible.

Price $43.45 PEG 0.8 P/E 18.5 ROIC 28.9% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings (July 2026) where ARR growth rate and cloud ACV bookings are disclosed — if management confirms the subscription conversion inflection is driving durable 20%+ ARR growth rather than one-time catch-up, consensus estimates revision follows and the PEG of 0.8 normalizes toward 1.2-1.4x, implying 50%+ upside. Watch The ⚠RevGap flag (+97%) signals analyst estimates significantly above the revenue track record — if Q2 earnings miss consensus ARR targets even modestly, the re-rating thesis delays 6-12 months and the stock retraces; this is a high-reward, execution-dependent story.
5. DUOL Duolingo, Inc. · Technology — Consumer SaaS HIGH WITHIN WEEKS Fast Grower

"97 million daily active users opening the app more than Instagram — at 19% of its 52-week high with PEG 0.7"

The market threw Duolingo out with the 2021-2022 growth stock bath and never updated its model for what actually happened: the company grew revenue 39-41% for two consecutive years, reached 36% FCF margins, and launched a premium Max tier at $168/year (3x the base price) that is the purest pricing power story in consumer software. At 19% of its 52-week high with PEG 0.7 and 74% DCF margin of safety, the market is pricing this as a broken growth story when it's actually a profitable, accelerating compounder. The key insight: less than 3% of 500M+ registered users are paying subscribers — the monetization story is in the first inning, not the ninth.

Price $103.47 PEG 0.7 P/E 11.1 ROIC 9.4% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings (August 2026) — if the Duolingo Max tier penetration rate increases from ~1% toward 2-3% of DAUs and family plan international rollout shows accelerating attach, the revenue trajectory forces analysts to revise 5-year EG estimates upward from the current 16%, materially compressing the PEG below 0.5. Watch ROIC is only 9% currently — this is the one genuine quality concern; the thesis requires operating leverage to drive ROIC toward 15%+ as subscriber revenue scales; if Q2 shows operating margin stagnation despite revenue growth, the quality compounder story weakens materially.
6. MNDY monday.com Ltd. · Technology — Work Management SaaS HIGH ACT NOW Turnaround

"Just crossed into GAAP profitability with 40% net cash on the balance sheet — the market is still pricing the old loss-making story"

MNDY sits at 21% of its 52-week high with forward PE of 8.4x versus trailing 28.5x — that compression is not a valuation trap, it's the arithmetic of a business that just crossed into GAAP profitability while the market hasn't noticed. With 89% gross margins, 26% FCF margins, FCF conversion of 2.7x, and 40% net cash acting as a floor, this is the profitability inflection mispricing story with the clearest structural floor in this entire candidate set. The market is still pricing the 2022-era cash-burning growth story, not the 2026 profitable enterprise platform with multi-product CRM, Dev, and Service expansion driving NRR above 115%.

Price $66.38 PEG 1.6 P/E 28.5 ROIC -0.1% Catalyst Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) — enterprise seat expansion data and AI monetization ARR contribution will be disclosed; if enterprise customer count growth exceeds 15% YoY and AI tier attach rate is quantified, the market re-rates from 8.4x forward PE toward a more appropriate 18-22x for a profitable, 27%-growing SaaS platform. Watch Revenue deceleration from +27% toward +20% or below over two quarters would signal the post-COVID work management TAM is saturating faster than the multi-product expansion can compensate — watch enterprise net revenue retention rate as the leading indicator.
7. DLO DLocal Limited · Technology — Emerging Markets FinTech HIGH WITHIN MONTHS Fast Grower

"The only payment rail in 40+ frontier markets, growing 47%, with 29% ROIC and PEG 0.9 — Wall Street barely knows it exists"

DLocal is covered by fewer than 8 analysts, headquartered in Uruguay, and processes payments in markets that CNBC has never mentioned — and that obscurity is the entire opportunity. The business is structurally superior to any EM payments alternative: local acquiring licenses in 40+ frontier markets take 5+ years to replicate, 29% ROIC proves the economics are real, FCF conversion of 2.1x (despite only 37% gross margins) confirms real cash generation, and the overhang from a 2023 short-seller attack has been answered by every subsequent quarterly result. The Fortune 500 EM payment routing opportunity — routing Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia transactions through DLocal instead of expensive bank wires — has barely started.

Price $13.42 PEG 0.9 P/E 20.3 ROIC 28.5% Catalyst Next quarterly earnings (Q2 2026) — if US and European enterprise merchant onboarding volumes show acceleration (evidence that the Fortune 500 routing shift is beginning), the market re-rates from PEG 0.9 toward 1.5-1.8x on 47% growth, implying 60-90% upside; specific watch: Total Payment Volume (TPV) growth versus net revenue growth spread as an indicator of take-rate stability. Watch Gross margin at 37% is the key vulnerability — any regulatory change requiring higher local acquiring costs or a corridor-level pricing war with a regional competitor could compress net revenue per transaction; the 2023 short-seller attack specifically targeted take-rate transparency, and this must be monitored quarterly.
8. PTCT PTC Therapeutics, Inc. · Healthcare — Rare Disease MEDIUM WITHIN MONTHS Fast Grower

"Revenue up 115%, operating margins at 49%, ROIC at 37%, and the stock trades at PE 8.7x — the market thinks this is a one-time spike"

PTCT just delivered a financial transformation that almost never happens in biopharma: revenue grew +115% (from -14% prior year), ROIC jumped to 37%, operating margin hit 49%, and net cash is 25% of market cap — yet the stock trades at PE 8.7x, the valuation of a melting ice cube. The market's error is treating Translarna/Ataluren and Upstaza as one-time regulatory events rather than what they actually are: the beginning of a rare disease royalty-like platform with orphan pricing power, high gross margins (91%), and a patient population that cannot discontinue treatment. At PE 8.7x with 49% operating margins, 37% ROIC, and 25% net cash, this is one of the most asymmetric setups in healthcare.

Price $71.27 PEG — P/E 8.7 ROIC 37.4% Catalyst Q2 2026 earnings (August 2026) — if management provides guidance showing Translarna revenue run-rate and Upstaza patient enrollment continuing at current pace, analyst consensus EPS revisions should follow; any strategic interest disclosure or partnership announcement would be a binary upside catalyst given the PE 8.7x floor. Watch The RGprev=-14% is the critical context — this was a commercial-stage company losing revenue before the inflection; if Translarna faces accelerated label restriction in Europe (EMA has previously challenged the conditional approval) or Upstaza patient recruitment disappoints, the revenue growth thesis reverses sharply.

⚠ Key Risks

  • Fed re-acceleration risk: if CPI re-accelerates above 3.5% in the May 2026 print, rate cut expectations collapse further and growth multiples face another compression leg — most damaging to MNDY and DUOL which still carry elevated trailing PEs
  • Enterprise IT budget freeze: any macro shock (tariff escalation, credit event) that causes CFOs to pause Q3 software renewals would delay the ARR re-acceleration thesis for PEGA, PATH, and MNDY — watch Q1 2026 earnings commentary for renewal rate language
  • EM FX and geopolitical volatility: DLO and RELY are directly exposed to corridor-level FX disruption and frontier market regulatory risk — a significant devaluation in a key EM currency (BRL, MXN, PHP) could compress net revenue per transaction materially in a single quarter

VIX at 18.4 signals caution but not panic — well below the 25+ threshold that historically marks genuine capitulation; the normal yield curve (+53bps) and 4.3% unemployment rule out imminent recession, but 10Y at 4.26% with CPI still sticky at 3.29% means multiple expansion for growth stocks requires earnings beats to drive re-rating, not just macro improvement. The setup favors patient, catalyst-driven entry in quality names rather than broad market aggression.

AI MARKET SYNOPSIS

The market is making two classic errors simultaneously: pricing high-quality, cash-generative compounders as if they are speculative growth stories (QLYS, DOCS, HALO), and anchoring on trailing PE multiples for businesses at profitability inflection points where forward PE has collapsed 60-80% (MNDY, HALO, RDDT). The real opportunity is in names where ROIC > 20%, FCF margins > 30%, and a specific 1-6 month catalyst exists — but the stock sits at 20-55% of its 52-week high because macro fear has indiscriminately punished all growth.

🌍 GEOPOLITICAL & MACRO CONTEXT

The 10Y at 4.26% and Fed Funds at 3.64% with CPI still at 3.29% means the Fed remains constrained — cuts are not imminent, which compresses duration risk in pure-growth names but disproportionately rewards businesses with high current FCF yields (QLYS at 10%, HALO at 8%, DOCS at 7%) that do not need rate relief to perform. The yield curve is now normal at +53bps (10Y-2Y), which historically signals the end of recession risk rather than the beginning, yet VIX at 18.4 reflects lingering caution — that gap between improving fundamentals and elevated fear is precisely where alpha lives. Unemployment at 4.3% is healthy, consumer spending on digital services remains intact, and enterprise IT budgets are recovering post-2025 breach cycle — the macro backdrop for quality SaaS and healthcare platforms is better than the beaten-down price levels suggest.
